growth_records.cite: Schöne et al. Deconstructing Methanosarcina acetivorans into an acetogenic archaeon. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2022). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2113853119. [PMC8764690]
growth_records.snippet: Table 1. Growth parameters of M. acetivorans on CO ... M42: Growth rate [h-1] 0.043 ± 0.002; Doubling time [h] 16.0 ± 0.6; ... Yield [g/mol acetate] 20.5 ± 3.6
growth_records.notes: Growth rate and doubling time directly reported in Table 1 for strain M42 grown anaerobically on CO (150 kPa headspace, sole energy substrate) in P-HS medium (60 mM Pipes, pH 7.4). Methanosarcina acetivorans is an obligate anaerobic methanogen/archaeon cultivated in sealed anaerobic (Balch) tubes; oxygen condition inferred as anaerobic from organism physiology and cultivation method (not an explicit 'anaerobic' statement in the CO-growth section, so treat with slight caution). Yield values in table are g biomass per mol substrate/product (stoichiometric yields), not specific rates (mmol/gDW/h), so NOT reported here as a computed secretion rate -- listed as reported yield only, rate left null per strict-computation rule (no specific qP value or biomass/time data available to convert).

growth_records.cite: Schöne et al. Deconstructing Methanosarcina acetivorans into an acetogenic archaeon. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2022). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2113853119. [PMC8764690]
growth_records.snippet: Table 1: MKOmtr3 (+2 mM MeOH): Growth rate [h-1] 0.040 ± 0.001; Doubling time [h] 17.4 ± 0.6
growth_records.notes: Growth rate and doubling time directly reported in Table 1 for strain MKOmtr3 grown anaerobically on CO plus 2 mM methanol supplement in P-HS medium (pH 7.4). Yield values n.d. for this strain in the table.

growth_records.cite: Schöne et al. Deconstructing Methanosarcina acetivorans into an acetogenic archaeon. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (2022). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2113853119. [PMC8764690]
growth_records.snippet: Table 1: MKOmtrhdr: Growth rate [h-1] 0.032 ± 0.001; Doubling time [h] 21.6 ± 0.4; Yield [g/mol acetate] 14.8 ± 4.1
growth_records.notes: Growth rate and doubling time directly reported in Table 1 for strain MKOmtrhdr grown anaerobically on CO alone in P-HS medium (pH 7.4). Yield in g/mol acetate reported as stoichiometric yield only, not converted to a rate.

growth_records.strain: engineered M. acetivorans expressing ANME-1 Mcr

up.method: substrate for reverse methanogenesis; specific uptake not stated

sec.method: produced by further-engineered strain; only yield reported

growth_records.cite: Liu et al. Methanoperedenaceae archaea: a 20-year research journey. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-69699-0. [PMC13046784]
growth_records.snippet: introduction of ANME-1 methyl-coenzyme M reductase (Mcr) genes into the methanogen host Methanosarcina acetivorans allowed this engineered organism to perform reverse methanogenesis, converting methane to acetate at a rate of 2.0 mmol L-1 d
growth_records.notes: Review-cited secondary values (refs 112,113), not primary to this paper. Acetate secretion = 2.0 mmol/L/d is VOLUMETRIC (per reactor volume per day), not biomass-specific (no gDW given), so no qS/qP per-gDW derivable. Lactate product yield = 0.1 mol lactate/mol methane (stated as ~10x aerobic route, ref 114). No growth rate or medium reported.

growth_records.medium: High-salt (HS) medium, bicarbonate-buffered, minus acetate, supplemented with ferrihydrite as electron acceptor and methane headspace

up.units: mol per mol CH4 (normalization basis of balance Eq.1)
up.method: substrate oxidized; growth tracked as protein increase dependent on methane consumption (SI Fig. S1)

up.units: mol Fe3+ reduced per mol CH4 oxidized
up.method: carbon/electron balance Eq.1; Fe(III) reduction measured as Fe(II) increase (ferrozine assay)

up.units: mol per mol CH4 (net consumed in balance)
up.method: carbon/electron balance Eq.1 (CO2 appears on reactant side)

sec.method: carbon/electron balance Eq.1; identity confirmed by 13CH4 to H13COOH
carbon/electron balance Eq.1; identity confirmed by 13CH4 to 13CH3-12COOH and 13CH3-13COOH

growth_records.cite: Yan et al. Respiration-driven methanotrophic growth of diverse marine methanogens. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2023). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2303179120. [PMC10523532]
growth_records.snippet: The doubling time for ferrihydrite-dependent methanotrophic growth of M. acetivorans C2A was 29 d compared to 12 d for ferrihydrite-dependent acetotrophic growth. ... the Y_ATP (g dry weight/mole) is ... 14.4 for M. acetivorans C2A ... The
growth_records.notes: Methanotrophic (reverse-methanogenesis) growth. mu = ln2/td; reported td = 29 d = 29*24 = 696 h; mu = 0.6931/696 = 9.96e-4 /h. Doubling_time_h = 696. Stoichiometric fluxes per mol CH4 from balance Eq.1: CH4 + 0.04 CO2 + 0.16 NH4Cl + 4.14 Fe3+ + 0.79 H2O = 0.18 CH2O2(formate) + 0.09 C2H4O2(acetate) + 4.14 Fe2+ + 4.14 H+ + 0.16 HCl + 0.68 CH1.77O0.49N0.24(biomass). Y_ATP=14.4 gDW/mol ATP and Y_DX=0.68 C-mol/C-mol taken verbatim. Specific rates in mmol/gDW/h not derivable (protein-based biomass, no explicit gDW-time series). CO2 measured to increase (SI Fig. S5) but authors state not necessarily a product (bicarbonate release); balance treats CO2 as small net reactant (0.04).

growth_records.medium: High-salt (HS) medium, bicarbonate-buffered, minus acetate, supplemented with humic acids as electron acceptor and methane headspace

up.method: protein increase dependent on methane consumption; time course Fig.1A
humic acids served as electron acceptor supporting greater energy conservation than ferrihydrite

growth_records.cite: Yan et al. Respiration-driven methanotrophic growth of diverse marine methanogens. Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A (2023). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2303179120. [PMC10523532]
growth_records.snippet: Humic acids were more favorable than ferrihydrite for respiratory growth which shows that humic acids served as an electron acceptor and supported greater energy conservation compared to ferrihydrite. This trend held for ATP levels during g
growth_records.notes: Humic-acid-dependent methanotrophic growth of M. acetivorans C2A (humic acids 0.22 g/L). No numeric doubling time/growth rate reported for the humic-acid condition (the 29 d value is stated only for the ferrihydrite condition). Growth confirmed qualitatively (protein/ATP increase, Fig.1C,D); formate and acetate are end products. No quantitative flux derivable.

growth_records.strain: C2A, engineered M. a 3fk (pWM321 + glf + glk from Zymomonas mobilis)

up.method: HPLC (Rezex ROA-Organic Acid H+, RI detector)

mfa.note: 13C6-glucose label detected in all carbons of G6P, FBP, pyruvate and AcCoA at steady state (36 h)

mfa.reaction_or_metabolite: TCA intermediates (citrate, isocitrate, succinate, malate, oxaloacetate)
mfa.note: all labeled from 13C6-glucose, indicating flux from AcCoA into TCA for biosynthesis
13C-labeled CH4 detected only in M. a 3fk, confirming glucose-derived carbon reaches CH4

growth_records.cite: Ma, Jing-Ya et al. A hybrid photocatalytic system enables direct glucose utilization for methanogenesis. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (PNAS) (2024). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2317058121. [PMC10823229]
growth_records.snippet: the CH4 yield of M. a 3fk was 4.7 mmol/OD after 58 h, 33% significantly higher than that of M. a 321 ... the glucose concentration in M. a 3fk culture gradually decreased from 14 mM to 9.8 mM after 58 h
growth_records.notes: Engineered archaeon; glucose is a non-native substrate, methanol (50 mM) is the primary methanogenic substrate. Glucose uptake volumetric rate = (14-9.8) mM / 58 h = 4.2 mmol/L / 58 h = 0.0724 mmol/L/h. Cannot convert to mmol/gDW/h: biomass reported only as OD600, no gDW conversion given. CH4 reported as a per-OD yield (4.7 mmol/OD), not a time-normalized specific rate. Full HS salt composition is in SI Appendix Table S1/S5 (not in main text); main-text-stated components listed. Stock strain from Japan Collection of Microorganisms; base HS medium normally with 125 mM methanol, modified here to 50 mM methanol + 14 mM glucose. n=5 biological replicates.

growth_records.strain: C2A, control M. a 321 (pWM321 empty shuttle vector)
growth_records.cite: Ma, Jing-Ya et al. A hybrid photocatalytic system enables direct glucose utilization for methanogenesis. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (PNAS) (2024). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2317058121. [PMC10823229]
growth_records.snippet: the CH4 yield of M. a 3fk was 4.7 mmol/OD after 58 h, 33% significantly higher than that of M. a 321 ... no obvious glucose consumption was observed in M. a 321 culture during the experiments
growth_records.notes: Control strain lacks glf/glk so cannot import glucose; methanol is sole methanogenic substrate. M. a 321 CH4 yield derived from reported relation: M. a 3fk (4.7 mmol/OD) is 33% higher than M. a 321, so M. a 321 = 4.7 / 1.33 = 3.53 mmol/OD. Glucose uptake ~0 (explicitly 'no obvious glucose consumption'). No gDW conversion available. n=5.

growth_records.strain: C2A, engineered M. a 3fk with CdS nanoparticles (3FK-CdS biohybrid)
growth_records.cite: Ma, Jing-Ya et al. A hybrid photocatalytic system enables direct glucose utilization for methanogenesis. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (PNAS) (2024). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2317058121. [PMC10823229]
growth_records.snippet: glucose consumption by 3FK-CdS biohybrid was significantly enhanced by 56% in comparison to M. a 3fk after 2 d illumination ... a maximum CH4 yield from 3FK-CdS was 5.4 mmol/OD, 26 and 35% significantly higher than that from M. a 3fk and 32
growth_records.notes: Photocatalytic biohybrid (biotic + abiotic CdS). Glucose consumption is 56% higher than M. a 3fk. Base M. a 3fk consumed 14-9.8=4.2 mmol/L over 58 h; 3FK-CdS = 4.2 x 1.56 = 6.55 mmol/L, volumetric qS = 6.55/58 = 0.113 mmol/L/h. Note text says 'after 2 d illumination' while Fig 4A states 58 h culture; used 58 h. CH4 max yield 5.4 mmol/OD (given directly). Cannot separate abiotic vs biotic electron contribution; part of enhancement is photocatalytic. No gDW conversion. n=5.

growth_records.strain: C2A, control M. a 321 with CdS nanoparticles (321-CdS biohybrid)
growth_records.cite: Ma, Jing-Ya et al. A hybrid photocatalytic system enables direct glucose utilization for methanogenesis. Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America (PNAS) (2024). DOI:10.1073/pnas.2317058121. [PMC10823229]
growth_records.snippet: a maximum CH4 yield from 3FK-CdS was 5.4 mmol/OD, 26 and 35% significantly higher than that from M. a 3fk and 321-CdS ... CH4 production has no obvious difference between 321-CdS and M. a 321 under illumination
growth_records.notes: Control biohybrid (no glucose transporter). CH4 yield derived: 3FK-CdS (5.4 mmol/OD) is 35% higher than 321-CdS, so 321-CdS = 5.4 / 1.35 = 4.0 mmol/OD. CH4 production no different from M. a 321 (light gives no glucose-driven benefit without glf/glk). Glucose uptake ~0. No gDW conversion. n=5.

growth_records.cite: Thor, ShengShee et al. Genome-Scale Metabolic Modeling of Archaea Lends Insight into Diversity of Metabolic Function. Archaea (2017). DOI:10.1155/2017/9763848. [PMC5241448]
growth_records.snippet: The models were simulated using experimental growth substrate uptakes of MeOH:20, Acetate:7, and CO:11.6 mmol/gDCW/hr.
growth_records.notes: Methanol uptake = 20 mmol/gDCW/h, stated verbatim in Figure 4 caption as an EXPERIMENTAL growth-substrate uptake used to constrain the M. acetivorans models. This is a review; the value is cited from primary literature (Figure 4 caption cites experimental growth rates refs 30-40, yields refs 40-42, CH4 production refs 33/42-44). No temperature, pH, growth-mode or numeric growth rate/yield given in text. Medium named as Wolfe's medium in text but full composition not provided.

growth_records.cite: Thor, ShengShee et al. Genome-Scale Metabolic Modeling of Archaea Lends Insight into Diversity of Metabolic Function. Archaea (2017). DOI:10.1155/2017/9763848. [PMC5241448]
growth_records.snippet: The models were simulated using experimental growth substrate uptakes of MeOH:20, Acetate:7, and CO:11.6 mmol/gDCW/hr.
growth_records.notes: CO uptake = 11.6 mmol/gDCW/h, stated verbatim in Figure 4 caption as an EXPERIMENTAL growth-substrate uptake used to constrain the M. acetivorans models. Review article; value cited from primary literature (Figure 4 caption cites experimental growth rates refs 30-40, yields refs 40-42, CH4 production refs 33/42-44). Note: a separate TMA (trimethylamine) uptake of 6.77 mmol/gDCW/h is NOT experimental (text states 'experimental TMA uptake rates were not available'; it was fitted from iST807 to experimental growth rates on TMA) and is therefore excluded. No temperature/pH/growth-mode or numeric growth rate/yield in text. Wolfe's medium named; full composition not given.
